Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'm 29, working on the side with limited resources, so I couldn't afford the luxury of a neat schedule. I'd usually cram study sessions into whatever gaps I could find—late nights, early mornings before work, weekends when I wasn't completely exhausted. I learn best by doing things hands-on, so I'd often spend my 26 hours a week actually working through problems rather than just reading. The routine was more reactive than planned, responding to whatever was most urgent.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on deadlines and life circumstances. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then I'd have these intense bursts where I'd study for hours straight because something was due. Being a kinesthetic learner, I needed blocks of time to actually practice and apply concepts, which wasn't always possible with my schedule. The regularity just wasn't there—I'd go days without logging in, then suddenly be all over the course materials when panic set in.
Q3: Usually it was the creeping dread of a deadline that got me to log in. I'd get an email notification or suddenly remember an assignment was coming up, and that anxiety would push me to check the LMS. Sometimes I logged in just to see if there was anything urgent I'd missed since my attendance was so sporadic. The gamified tasks actually helped sometimes—earning points or completing challenges gave me a small dopamine hit that made logging in feel slightly less tedious when I was bored with the regular content.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a real routine for me. There were brief periods where I'd check in more regularly, especially when the gamified elements were introduced and I got competitive about completing challenges. But those moments were fleeting. My life circumstances—working with low resources, managing anxiety, being older than most students—meant the LMS was always something I accessed out of necessity rather than habit.
Q5: The hands-on practice exercises and interactive elements were the most valuable for me as a kinesthetic learner. I actually retained information when I could manipulate things, work through problems, or engage with simulations. The gamified tasks helped too because they broke down larger concepts into manageable challenges that felt achievable. Discussion posts were occasionally useful when peers shared practical applications or real-world examples that made abstract concepts click.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the video content—I'd watch maybe 13 minutes on average because sitting passively just doesn't work for my brain. I'd zone out completely, feeling bored, and realize I hadn't absorbed anything. Reading materials I'd often download once and never return to on the LMS itself. The lengthy theoretical discussions in forums I'd skip entirely unless they were directly relevant to an assignment I was working on.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate practical application or when it directly contributed to my grade. Given my limited resources and packed schedule, I had to be ruthless about prioritization. The gamified tasks worked because they gave instant feedback and a sense of progress. If something felt like busywork or pure theory without application, my motivation—which was already low—would completely tank.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With 16 online courses to juggle and my already stretched resources, anything labeled 'optional' automatically went to the bottom of my priority list. Required activities got done because they had to, even if I was just going through the motions. The optional stuff might have been enriching, but when you're operating in crisis mode, enrichment takes a backseat to survival.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline and invisible to the LMS. I'd download materials, PDFs, practice problems and work through them on paper or on my own devices without any internet connection. I'd use physical notebooks to sketch out concepts and work through problems by hand—that kinesthetic approach really helped things stick. External YouTube tutorials, free resources, library books—whatever I could access without paying extra given my financial constraints.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened away from the screen. I'd print out problem sets and work through them at my kitchen table, or I'd practice techniques during downtime at work. There were entire study sessions where I'd have a single PDF open for hours, working through it methodically, but the LMS would only show that I downloaded one file. My peer interactions number of 25 doesn't capture the study group meetups where we'd work through concepts together in person.
Q11: All the time. I'd log in, download everything I needed for the week in one session, then not touch the LMS again until the next deadline approached. My internet access isn't always reliable given my situation, so having offline copies was essential. I'd spend hours with those downloaded materials, annotating them, working through exercises, but none of that showed up in any engagement metrics.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where engagement became nearly impossible. My anxiety would spike—hitting that high level—and I'd freeze up, unable to even look at course materials. Financial stress, work demands, feeling overwhelmed by having 16 courses to manage simultaneously—it all compounded. Despite my high resilience, there were weeks where just surviving took all my energy, and the course became background noise I couldn't tune into.
Q13: Constantly. The cognitive load of managing so many courses with limited resources meant I was always in triage mode on the LMS. When work got demanding or bills were due, my LMS usage would drop to zero. Fatigue was a huge factor—after long days, I'd log in with good intentions but find myself just staring at the screen, too exhausted to actually engage. The confusion from missing lectures due to my 14% attendance made it even harder to catch up when I did have energy.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered elsewhere, thinking about work or money or just feeling bored and disconnected. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something. My low engagement level reflected this—I was technically present but mentally checked out, going through motions without genuine learning happening.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety would build up around difficult assignments, and instead of tackling them, I'd avoid them until the pressure became unbearable. It's a frustrating cycle—the avoidance made the anxiety worse, which made me avoid more. Sometimes I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I had more energy, but tomorrow would come with its own crises. My assignment completion rate of 81.9% shows I eventually got most things done, but rarely on my own timeline.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire operating system. Without them, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all given my low motivation. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become hyper-focused, cramming weeks of work into days or hours. My response time averaging almost 23 hours shows I was usually working right up against those deadlines. It wasn't healthy, but it was functional—deadlines created the external pressure my internal motivation couldn't provide.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When it was constructive and specific, it actually helped me understand what to focus on, which was valuable given my limited time. But generic feedback or low grades without explanation would tank my already fragile mood and make me want to disengage entirely. The gamified task feedback was more helpful—immediate, clear, and actionable. Waiting days for assignment feedback while anxious about my performance was torture.
Q18: The course structure was both a lifeline and a burden. Having clear deadlines and requirements gave me something concrete to work toward, which I needed with my low motivation. But the sheer volume—16 online courses—meant the structure became overwhelming rather than supportive. The gamified elements helped break things into smaller, more manageable pieces. Without that structure, I would have been completely lost, but with it, I still felt like I was constantly drowning.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier—they tapped into my competitive side and gave me quick wins when everything else felt overwhelming. What made it harder was content that required consistent, incremental engagement rather than intensive bursts. The assumption that students could attend regularly and build knowledge sequentially didn't account for people like me who were juggling multiple life demands. More flexibility in how and when to demonstrate learning would have helped enormously.
Q20: I think those students might be like me in some ways—logging in, clicking around, maybe watching videos passively or scrolling through materials without really engaging. High activity could mean someone is lost and searching for information they can't find, or anxiously checking and rechecking without actually producing work. It could also be someone who's struggling with the material and spending lots of time without making progress. Activity doesn't equal productivity or understanding.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience pretty closely. My LMS activity was low—minimal video watching, sporadic logins—but I still passed with decent assignment completion. The learning was happening, just not where the system could see it. I was downloading materials, studying offline, working through problems on paper, meeting with peers in person. Someone looking at my log data would think I was barely trying, but I was putting in those 26 hours a week, just invisibly.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend on pages. That's useful for identifying students who've completely disappeared. But logs completely miss the quality of engagement, the offline work, the mental state behind the clicks. They can't tell if someone watched a video attentively or had it playing while scrolling their phone. They can't capture the anxiety that makes someone avoid logging in, or the productive studying happening with downloaded materials.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually wrestling with the material—not just consuming it passively, but working through problems, making connections, applying concepts to real situations. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, practicing. It doesn't require being online or visible in any system. Some of my most engaged learning moments happened with a pencil and paper at 2 AM, working through a concept until it finally clicked. Engagement is internal and active, not just showing up.
Q24: The LMS data would miss almost everything that matters about my engagement. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with downloaded materials, the anxiety that kept me away some weeks, or the resilience that brought me back. It wouldn't capture my offline study sessions, my kinesthetic learning approach, or the real understanding I developed through hands-on practice. It would just show a struggling student with low engagement metrics, not someone fighting hard to learn despite significant obstacles and limited resources.
